sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports difficulty swallowing solids for approximately four to five weeks, experiencing pain when attempting to swallow them. He describes a sensation of needing assistance to push solids down, particularly with larger items like sandwiches. He denies coughing, choking, or vomiting blood. He reports no weight loss or chest pain. He checks his blood sugars approximately 80% of the time, primarily in the morning. His home blood pressure readings are occasionally higher than the office readings. He reports no nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea related to the metformin.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Physical exam reveals no neck masses or lymphadenopathy. The thyroid is normal in size and feel. The throat appears normal with no redness or swelling. Lung exam is clear. Heart exam is normal with no murmurs. Abdominal exam reveals mild epigastric tenderness. Neurological exam is unremarkable. Blood pressure is 120/50 mmHg in the office.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The physician suspects a throat narrowing or stricture, potentially causing the swallowing difficulties. A scope is indicated to evaluate the esophagus.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: The physician recommends starting Prilosec 20mg once daily for potential reflux contributing to the swallowing issues. The physician recommends frequent chewing of food, especially with solid foods. The physician orders a repeat Hemoglobin A1c and additional blood work including kidney function. The physician orders an ophthalmology referral for diabetic retinopathy screening. The physician will continue metformin. A refill for norvasc will be sent to the patient's pharmacy. Follow-up will be scheduled in one month to assess response to treatment and review laboratory results.
